text:
Lord Ebbing washed down his grilled cheese with a sip of coffee. “Exactly. They realized we’d spotted the pattern, so they decided there wouldn’t be a pattern. You can’t tell those bastards at the shipping companies that, of course, or they’ll tear us to pieces. As they did to you when you suggested anything that might cut their profits.”

“Then what do we do?”

“We do nothing. Mr. Powlett was right. If turning a profit matters that much more to them than waiting for the proper windows, then what’s left is to make our ships tougher, our sailors hardier, and our weapons more effective, so that when we meet these beasts, we finally win. You weren’t wrong to say so in there, and some agreed with you, even if Lord Denver was the loudest. If Mr. Powlett puts a proposal before Parliament to place soldiers on merchant ships, I’ll support it. Then I’ll have the sea monster brought home and hang its severed head in the harbor to warn off its kind.”

Caleb laughed at how carried away Lord Ebbing had become. His teacher was clearly energized by finding a nemesis in the deep, and he couldn’t deny it stirred his own imagination too.
